dossier.tagline: Available darkness, held on a long color negative.
dossier.portrait: Cinema 500T belongs to practical lamps, blue windows, wet pavement, and faces emerging from the bottom of the curve. It is fast but not frantic: the dye structure stays measured, the shoulder makes room for bulbs and signs, and tungsten balance lets warm household light feel like illumination rather than a technical mistake.

chapters.title: A multilayer negative designed for the scan
chapters.lede: VISION3 500T uses multiple color-sensitive records, advanced tabular-grain technology, and Kodak’s Dye Layering Technology. DLT is specifically described as reducing grain in shadows, where a fast stock and a digital scan are most likely to reveal it.

details.label: Image-forming material
details.value: Cyan, magenta, and yellow dye records created during ECN-2 color development.

details.label: Dye Layering Technology
details.value: Kodak’s proprietary layer architecture is designed to improve shadow signal-to-noise and visible grain.

details.label: Rem-jet
details.value: A carbon-backed anti-halation and transport layer softened and removed at the beginning of ECN-2 processing.

details.label: Grainlab model
details.value: Fine-to-medium dye clouds, tabular core-shell behavior, restrained chroma variation, and exposure-aware shadow texture.

notes: Motion-picture grain should feel stable and organic frame to frame; a tiled, repeating texture breaks the illusion immediately.
The fast rating comes from emulsion engineering, not simply enlarging every crystal uniformly.

chapters.title: Highlights get the penthouse; shadows pay rent
chapters.lede: Kodak’s published exposure diagrams emphasize substantial room above a white card and below a black card. The exact usable range depends on flare, lens, processing, scan, and acceptance threshold, but the design priority is clear: hold extremes for postproduction.

details.label: Tungsten baseline
details.value: EI 500 under 3200 K illumination without a color-conversion filter.

details.label: Daylight correction
details.value: EI 320 with an 85 filter converts daylight toward the stock’s tungsten balance.

details.label: Uncorrected daylight
details.value: A blue-biased negative that can be used deliberately, with channel balance and exposure considered in the grade.

details.label: Shadow exposure
details.value: Thin shadows reveal grain and color noise first; rating at EI 320 is a common creative overexposure strategy, not a new ISO.

notes: Expose the negative for the shadow color you need, then control practical lamps and windows with lighting, filtration, and the final grade.
A bright bulb may fit the curve while still dominating the scene aesthetically—latitude is not composition.

chapters.title: Tungsten is a point of view
chapters.lede: At 3200 K the color records sit near their intended neutral relationship. Daylight windows go blue; sodium, fluorescent, LED, and firelight each arrive with their own spectral discontinuities. The stock’s attraction is how those sources coexist rather than disappear into one white balance.

details.label: Skin
details.value: Natural under controlled tungsten, with warm practicals allowed to remain visibly warm.

details.label: Mixed light
details.value: Blue daylight and amber interiors separate strongly—a cinematic relationship often preserved rather than neutralized.

details.label: Greens
details.value: Fluorescent and discharge sources may need filtration or selective grading because spectrum, not CCT alone, controls the cast.

details.label: Night color
details.value: The negative can hold saturated signs and dim environmental color in one scan when exposure is adequate.

notes: Do not globally cool every image and call it 500T. The stock is a capture system whose balance becomes visible through actual source spectra.
Keep one believable neutral in the grade so the mixed-light relationships retain meaning.

chapters.title: A laboratory negative, not C-41 with movie branding
chapters.lede: ECN-2 begins with prebath and rem-jet removal, then uses CD-3 color development before stop, bleach, fix, wash, final rinse, and drying. The process and its aim differ from still-photo C-41 even though both end in dye-image color negatives.

details.label: Developer
details.value: ECN-2 color developer reduces exposed silver halide while oxidized CD-3 forms image dyes with incorporated couplers.

details.label: Bleach / fix
details.value: Bleach converts metallic silver to removable silver halide; fixer converts it to soluble complexes removed in wash.

details.label: Scan posture
details.value: Capture a wide-gamut, high-bit log-style master with the full negative range before display contrast.

details.label: Still-photo caution
details.value: Use a lab equipped for rem-jet and ECN-2. Cross-processing changes color, contrast, backing removal, and archival assumptions.

notes: A cinematic look is a chain: stock, lens, exposure, lighting, ECN-2, scan transform, and grade.
Grainlab emulates the finished image behavior; it does not reproduce a particular scanner matrix or Kodak print film path.

bestFor: Night streets, practical-lit interiors, stage work, blue-hour exteriors, and scenes built around mixed color temperature.
Wide-brightness compositions that need a grading-friendly negative.
Portraits where the room’s practical light should remain part of the skin-tone story.
Still images that should feel cut from a motion sequence rather than styled with a generic teal-orange LUT.
watchFor: Sending rem-jet-backed stock through a lab that expects ordinary C-41 film.
Underexposing faces and expecting push or scan gain to restore missing color separation.
Neutralizing every mixed source until the tungsten-balanced atmosphere disappears.
Adding coarse static grain that ignores exposure and motion-film stability.
fieldNotes: Let the table lamp be amber, let the window be blue, and decide which darkness contains the person.
The negative is wide so the grade can be specific—not so the lighting can be absent.

sources.title: KODAK VISION3 500T Color Negative Film 5219 / 7219 resources
Using KODAK Kit Chemicals in Motion Picture Film Laboratories

dossier.disclaimer: Cinema 500T is an independent Grainlab rendering inspired by KODAK VISION3 500T technical literature. It is not a Kodak LUT, scanner characterization, spectral profile, or substitute for the stock and ECN-2 process.
